title: OpenAI Launches Full-Scale Effort to Patch Open-Source Bugs as It Takes on Anthropic's Mythos
gist: OpenAI launched 'Patch the Planet' with security firm Trail of Bits and vulnerability platforms HackerOne to offer free AI-assisted security consulting to open-source software maintainers, framing the effort as a strategic counter to the AI vulnerability-hunting wave that is overwhelming volunteer maintainers with AI-generated false reports. The announcement is part of a competitive cyber AI race with Anthropic, whose Fable 5/Mythos 5 models were pulled from market under Trump administration export controls, while OpenAI's GPT-5.5-Cyber outscores Anthropic's Mythos 5 on the CyberGym benchmark (85.6% vs. 83.8%). Five Eyes intelligence agencies issued an unusual joint warning that AI cyber capabilities will transform offense and defense within months, not years.

insights.claim: OpenAI launched 'Patch the Planet' in partnership with Trail of Bits, HackerOne, and Calif. to provide free AI-assisted security consulting to open-source maintainers, with Trail of Bits deploying 25 engineers—roughly one-fifth of its workforce—in an initial five-day sprint that already uncovered hundreds of bugs and produced dozens of patches.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Patch the Planet functions as both genuine public good and marketing to position OpenAI's AI security models as net-positive for the ecosystem—a reputational hedge ahead of IPO.
insights.quote: OpenAI and Trail of Bits say the project has already uncovered hundreds of bugs and produced dozens of patches in just its first week.

insights.claim: OpenAI has subsidized usage of its Codex Security scanner 'to the tune of 20 trillion tokens' for both open-source and private code bases, suggesting a deliberate loss-leader strategy to build market position in the AI security tooling space.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Codex Security could be monetized post-IPO once network effects and switching costs are established; 20 trillion tokens of subsidized usage represents substantial infrastructure spend to acquire enterprise customers.
insights.quote: OpenAI has been subsidizing usage for both open-source and private code 'to the tune of 20 trillion tokens.'

insights.claim: Trail of Bits CEO Dan Guido describes Patch the Planet as 'an internet-scale effort to help open-source software get ahead of AI bug-hunting tools,' framing the initiative as a defense against the same AI vulnerability-discovery capabilities that OpenAI and others are rapidly deploying.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: AI-accelerated vulnerability discovery creates a race condition: the same tools that expose flaws must also patch them or the net effect is a less secure internet; companies controlling both offense and defense tools capture the most value.
insights.quote: Patch the Planet is an internet-scale effort to help open-source software get ahead of AI bug-hunting tools

insights.claim: AI vulnerability-hunting tools are generating a flood of low-quality CVE reports ('slop CVEs') against open-source projects, overwhelming volunteer maintainers who have limited time and resources, making it harder to identify and prioritize genuinely critical flaws.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Open-source security is degrading under the weight of AI-generated noise; companies dependent on open-source infrastructure face rising supply-chain vulnerability risk—a tailwind for enterprise security vendors.
insights.quote: Maintainers 'do their work out of love of open source, and now they're stuck reviewing slop CVEs'

themes.theme: OpenAI vs. Anthropic cyber AI benchmark competition

insights.claim: OpenAI's GPT-5.5-Cyber scores 85.6% on the CyberGym benchmark, outperforming Anthropic's Mythos 5 at 83.8%—a narrow but publicly highlighted lead that OpenAI is using competitively even though both models are restricted to limited 'Trusted Access' programs rather than public release.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: Benchmark positioning matters for enterprise procurement decisions even in restricted-access programs; a 1.8 percentage point edge on CyberGym becomes marketing material as both companies pursue IPOs.
insights.quote: OpenAI points out that the model scores 85.6 percent on the benchmark assessment known as CyberGym, an improvement from a previous version of GPT-5.5-Cyber. The performance also beats Anthropic's Mythos 5, which scored 83.8 percent.

insights.claim: Anthropic was forced to pull its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models from the market earlier in June 2026 after the Trump administration imposed export controls citing fears that the AI cybersecurity capabilities embedded in the models were not adequately blocked—even though Anthropic had pre-emptively restricted biological and cybersecurity features.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: US government export controls on frontier AI cybersecurity models are now an active risk for AI companies pre-IPO; Anthropic's forced withdrawal demonstrates that self-imposed capability limits may not satisfy regulators.
insights.quote: Anthropic had to pull its new Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models off the market earlier this month amid fear from the Trump administration about AI cybersecurity capabilities.

insights.claim: With both Anthropic and OpenAI preparing for IPOs, competitive framing around AI cybersecurity capabilities continues regardless of which products are currently available to the public—suggesting regulatory setbacks like Anthropic's withdrawal only temporarily suppress the commercial race.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: The AI cyber race is structurally IPO-driven; expect both companies to continue publicizing benchmark scores and government partnerships as investor signals regardless of regulatory constraints.
insights.quote: with both Anthropic and OpenAI preparing for IPOs, competition clearly continues regardless of which products are currently on the market.

themes.theme: Five Eyes warning: AI cyber timeline is months not years

insights.claim: The Five Eyes intelligence alliance issued an unusual joint statement warning that 'frontier AI models are anticipated to exceed current industry expectations, fundamentally transforming both offensive and defensive cyber capabilities' within months, not years—the most explicit government acknowledgment yet of imminent AI-driven cyber escalation.
insights.speaker: Lily Hay Newman
insights.so_what: A Five Eyes joint warning is a rare, high-credibility signal; it validates accelerated spending on AI-native security tools and creates urgency for governments and enterprises to upgrade cyber defenses immediately.
insights.quote: frontier AI models are anticipated to exceed current industry expectations, fundamentally transforming both offensive and defensive cyber capabilities. The timeline is not years, it is months
